content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer NNN. Consider the sequence containing the integers 1,2,…,N1, 2, \dots, N1,2,…,N in increasing order (each exactly once). Find the length of the longest subarray in this sequence such that the bitwise AND of all elements in the subarray is **positive**.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT denoting the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains a single integer NNN on a single line.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a single line the length of the longest subarray that satisfy the given property.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the length of the longest subarray that satisfy the given property.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1 the only subarray is [1] (AND=1). For N=2 any subarray longer than 1 contains 1 whose AND becomes 0, so answer 1. For N=3 the subarray [2,3] has AND=2, length 2. For N=4 the best is [2,3] (AND=2) length 2. For N=7 the interval [4,7] has all numbers with the 3rd bit set, AND=4, length 4.

N=5: p=4, len1=2, len2=2, answer 2 (subarray [4,5]). N=8: p=8, len1=1, len2=4, answer 4 (subarray [4,7]). N=10: p=8, len1=3, len2=4, answer 4 (subarray [8,11] truncated to [8,10] gives length 3 but the interval [4,7] of length 4 still has positive AND, so answer 4).

solution.approach: The key insight is to find the highest power of two less than or equal to N. This is because any subarray with a length greater than this power of two will have at least one pair of numbers whose bitwise AND is zero. We can use bitwise operations to find this power of two efficiently.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log N) per test case, O(T log N) total.
